sanskrit_devanagari:
तमुवाच हृषीकेशः प्रहसन्निव भारत ।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये विषीदन्तमिदं वचः ॥ १० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
tam uvāca hṛṣīkeśaḥ prahasann iva bhārata |
senayor ubhayor madhye viṣīdantam idaṁ vacaḥ || 10 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.010 = MBh_06,024.010). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: O descendant of Bharata, at that time Kṛṣṇa, smiling, in the midst of both the armies, spoke the following words to the grief-stricken Arjuna.
O Dhritarashtra, thereafter, in the midst of both the armies, Shree Krishna smilingly spoke the following words to the grief-stricken Arjun.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The talk was going on between intimate friends, namely the Hṛṣīkeśa and the Guḍākeśa. As friends, both of them were on the same level, but one of them voluntarily became a student of the other. Kṛṣṇa was smiling because a friend had chosen to become a disciple.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.10 And here, the text commencing from ‘But seeing the army of the Pandavas’ (1.2) and ending with ‘(he) verily became silent, telling Him (Govinda), “I shall not fight”‘ is to be explained as revealing the cause of the origin of the defect in the from of sorrow, delusion, etc.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 'prahasann iva' (smiling, as if) carefully — the 'iva' (as if) softens the smile: it is not a smile of amusement at Arjuna's distress, but a smile that signals the teacher's settled state at the moment of beginning. The location matters too: 'in the midst of both armies' — the teaching happens in the field, not in retreat from it.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.9 – 2.10 Sanjaya said — Thus, the Lord, the Supreme Person, introduced the Sastra regarding the self for the sake of Arjuna — whose natural courage was lost due to love and compassion in a misplaced situation, who thought war to be unrighteous even though it was the highest duty for warriors (Ksat
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the smile as the Lord's compassionate readiness — the moment a teacher recognises that the student has finally crossed into a state where teaching can land. The smile is the recognition; the speech that follows is the response. The whole chapter unfolds out of this moment.

positions.rendering: the 'iva' (as if) is meaningful — not a smile of amusement, a smile of teacher's settled readiness

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both translators drop the 'iva' (as if) for English readability. The particle is doctrinally significant: it qualifies the smile, distancing it from amusement. Krishna is not smiling AT Arjuna; he is smiling in the way a settled teacher smiles when a long-deferred conversation is finally ready to begin.

literal_meaning: O Bhārata (Dhṛtarāṣṭra), to him — Arjuna in despair in the midst of the two armies — Hṛṣīkeśa (Krishna), as if smiling, spoke this speech.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The closing narrative verse before Krishna's teaching begins. Sanjaya describes the geometry of the moment: Krishna and Arjuna in the chariot, the two armies on either side, and Krishna smiling — but smiling 'iva' (as if), the qualifier that all major commentators take seriously. The smile is not amused; it is the settled teacher's readiness. The verse has minimal doctrinal content of its own, but it is the structural pivot of the chapter: 2.1-2.10 is Arjuna's case; 2.11 onward is Krishna's reply. This verse marks the transition.
